title: Gas-Phase Synthesis of Nanostructured Anatase TiO2
abstract:
Full Paper
Gas-Phase Synthesis of Nanostructured Anatase TiO2**
By Albert Goossens,* Edward-Louis Maloney, and Joop Schoonman
Nanostructured anatase titanium dioxide (TiO2) has been obtained using chemical vapor deposition (CVD). In order to
arrive at a fractal-type morphology, two different titanium precursors were injected simultaneously into a cold-wall CVD
system. With this method, a wide variety of morphologies can be obtained. For solar light harvesting, the structures of trees,
bushes, and grasses appear to be good choices. Small variations in deposition parameters have a dramatic effect on the struc-
ture of the deposits, which allows adequate optimization of nanostructures by gas-phase synthesis techniques.
Keywords: Chemical vapor deposition, Titanium dioxide, Nanostructured materials, Photovoltaic solar cells, Fractals
1. Introduction
At present, the science and technology of nanostructured
materials plays a dominant role in universities, research
institutes, and industrial laboratories. The discipline of
“nanoscience” deserves this attention owing to its potential
for uniting fundamental science (up to the level of quantum
physics) with materials technology. As a result of this multi-
disciplinary approach, the level of understanding of, and
the ability to control, atomic arrangements on the nano-
scale has increased tremendously in the past decade.
Nanostructured materials differ from macroscopic solids
because of the interrupted symmetry of atomic arrange-
ment. While in crystalline solids atomic order extends to
macroscopic dimensions, in nanostructured materials this is
no longer the case. Instead, a substantial fraction of the
atoms are located at or near an interface and experience a
potential different from atoms in bulk material. Surface
properties therefore predominate, but atoms further away
from the surface also experience modified conditions. Con-
sequently, material constants, such as the susceptibility to
electric and magnetic fields, optical absorption coefficient
and refractive index, heat capacity and Debye temperature,
become functions of the dimension. Accordingly, with the
advent of nanotechnology, constants that used to be looked
upon as inaccessible to engineering have become suscepti-
ble to human manipulation, giving rise to novel material
properties.
Nanostructured semiconductors form a class of new ma-
terials. For instance, porous silicon shows photo- and elec-
troluminescence despite the indirect bandgap of bulk
silicon. By tailoring the mesoscopic morphology, the wave-
length of emission (the bandgap) can be shifted. Another
example is the advent of nanostructured TiO2 films and
their use in a variety of devices such as electrochromic dis-
plays,[1] photovoltaic solar cells,[2] photocatalytic reactors,[3]
and rechargeable lithium ion batteries.[4] In this case the ex-
treme enhancement of the interfacial surface area, in com-
bination with the complicated electrostatic potential inside
the nanostructure, bring about the fundamental differences
in this form of TiO2 compared with its usual properties.
Nanostructured TiO2 is used in photocatalytic devices.[3]
Ceramic tiles coated with this material are currently pro-
duced for application in houses and cars. Decomposition of
virtually all poisonous molecules can be achieved, due to
the aggressive oxidizing power of photogenerated holes in
TiO2 in combination with a high density of active surface
sites in a nanostructured material. Nanostructured anatase
TiO2 has also been employed as the anode material in re-
chargeable lithium ion batteries.[4] In this case, the en-
hanced surface area speeds up the rate of intercalation of
the Li+ ions and offers a high storage capacity. Battery sys-
tems based on this material have already been commercial-
ized and may conquer a substantial fraction of the Li-bat-
tery market.
Surface area enhancement allows monolayer attachment
of functionalized molecules with a high volume concentra-
tion. Since these molecules are electrically accessible and
can be reduced or oxidized quickly, electrochromic devices
can be constructed, which change color upon the applica-
tion of a voltage.[1] Other types of functionalized molecules
are employed in photovoltaic solar cells. In this case mole-
cules with a strong and broad optical absorption, such as
ruthenium(II)
metal-to-ligand
charge-transfer
(MLCT)
complexes, are attached to the nanostructured TiO2 sur-
face. If the lifetime of the molecular excited state is long
Chem. Vap. Deposition 1998, 4, No. 3
WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 1998
0948-1907/98/0305-0109 $ 17.50+.50/0
109
Full Paper
–
[*] Dr. A. Goossens, E.-L. Maloney, Prof. J. Schoonman
Laboratory for Inorganic Chemistry
Delft University of Technology
PO Box 5045, 2600 GA Delft (The Netherlands)
[**] The authors thank the Netherlands Agency for Energy and the Envi-
ronment (Novem) and the European Union (Joule program) for finan-
cial
support of
the
investigations.
In addition, Dr. A Goossens
acknowledges the Royal Netherlands Academy of Arts and Sciences
for his fellowship.
 Full Paper
110
WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 1998
0948-1907/98/0305-0110 $ 17.50+.50/0
Chem. Vap. Deposition 1998, 4, No. 3
and the rate of regeneration is faster than the back flow of
electrons, efficient charge injection from the dye molecules
into the TiO2 conduction band is possible.[2] Owing to the
efforts of Grätzel and his group, solar cells with characteris-
tics comparable to amorphous silicon solar cells have been
developed with this novel device.[5] Commercialization of
this type of solar cell in the near future is anticipated.
It is worthwhile taking a closer look at the development
of this type of solar cell. The use of sensitizers to reduce
the optical absorption threshold of wide bandgap semicon-
ductors has been known for many years.[6] With the aid of
suitable sensitizing chromophores, liquid-junction photo-
voltaic (PV) devices can be constructed.[7] Until recently
however, solar cells based on this principle showed disap-
pointing efficiencies, and interest gradually faded away. In
the past decade, an elegant solution to the efficiency prob-
lem has been reported by Grätzel and co-workers.[2] In his
group, the principle of sensitization of wide-bandgap semi-
conductors has been converted into an operational PV cell
with an efficiency of 10 %. This type of solar cell makes use
of a nanostructured oxide semiconductor sensitized by a
monolayer of a chemically bound ruthenium(II) MLTC
complex. Nanostructured electrodes are necessary to pro-
vide a large surface-area-enhancement factor of 1000 with
respect to the geometrical surface area, which makes ade-
quate sunlight absorption by the chromophores feasible.
An electrolyte containing a I–/I3– redox couple provides the
intimate electrical contact to the nanostructured electrode.
Figure 1 presents, schematically, the operational principle
of this type of solar cell. While the unique optical proper-
ties of ruthenium(II) MLTC complexes had been exten-
sively studied long before their application in PV cells, the
concept of dye-sensitized nanostructured semiconductor
oxides could only be turned into an operational device with
the advent of nanostructured metal oxide semiconductor
electrodes. For a variety of reasons, the anatase form of
TiO2 appears to be most effective. Commercial powder
such as Degussa P25, or TiO2 colloids in a sol-gel process,
can be used as precursors in wet-chemical synthesis of por-
ous nanostructured titania electrodes. The morphology of
these electrodes is an agglomeration of spherical particles,
10–30 nm in diameter, and is independent of the type of
wet-chemical synthesis process; only the diameters of the
particles and their degree of agglomeration can be influ-
enced.
In this paper we report a novel synthesis of nanostruc-
tured anatase by means of CVD, which is a successful
method for thin film formation with specified composition,
microstructure, and morphology. In particular, it has been
shown by Takahashi et al.[8] that CVD synthesis of TiO2
shows a wide variety of possible morphologies. In their
study, morphological variations were linked to structural
changes making use of the anatase and rutile phases of
TiO2. Their approach was unsuitable for our purposes
since, for most applications mentioned above and in parti-
cular for solar cells, pure anatase is required. Therefore, in-
vestigations have been initiated in our laboratory to ex-
plore the possibility of utilizing CVD for the deposition of
nanostructured anatase TiO2. In this study, a large number
of different anatase TiO2 morphologies have been depos-
ited, analyzed, and tested for possible use in dye-sensitized
PV cells. Most of the films showed a disappointing cell effi-
ciency. It appeared that either the TiO2 films are too dense,
in which case the surface enhancement is too low, or the
films are too coarse, which limits the sinter activity be-
tween the particles, leading to a poor mechanical stability
and a high resistance to electron transport. In an attempt to
improve the performance of our films, CVD precursors, gas
flow rates, substrate temperatures, and a range of other de-
position conditions were varied. Finally, under white light
AM 1.5 irradiation, efficiencies of about 3 % could be es-
tablished, and when we looked at these films using scan-
ning electron microscopy, morphologies resembling trees,
bushes, and grasses appeared to be the best choice for har-
vesting sunlight.
2. Results and Discussion
Thin-film synthesis from reacting gases is attractive be-
cause of the extreme control one has over the growth me-
chanism and the morphology of the films. In the absence of
thermodynamic limitation and under laminar flow condi-
tions in the reactor, two different limiting regimes can be
discerned. First, at low substrate temperatures and high re-
actant concentrations, the film growth is limited by the rate
at which precursor molecules already adsorbed on the sur-
face react to form the solid product. This is the so-called
“reaction limited” regime. Second, at high substrate tem-
peratures and low reactant concentrations, the film growth
Fig. 1. Schematic drawing of the operational principle of dye-sensitized solar
cells. A nanostructured TiO2 electrode is sensitized for sunlight absorption
by a monolayer of a chromophore. Upon excitation of this chromophore,
electron injection into the conduction band of TiO2 takes place. Reduction
of the chromophore is taken care of by redox species in the electrolyte. The
electrons flow through the external circuit towards the back electrode, where
reduction of redox species takes place.

microstructure, and morphology. In particular, it has been

changes making use of the anatase and rutile phases of
TiO2. Their approach was unsuitable for our purposes
since, for most applications mentioned above and in parti-
cular for solar cells, pure anatase is required. Therefore, in-
vestigations have been initiated in our laboratory to ex-
plore the possibility of utilizing CVD for the deposition of
nanostructured anatase TiO2. In this study, a large number
of different anatase TiO2 morphologies have been depos-
ited, analyzed, and tested for possible use in dye-sensitized
PV cells. Most of the films showed a disappointing cell effi-
ciency. It appeared that either the TiO2 films are too dense,
in which case the surface enhancement is too low, or the
films are too coarse, which limits the sinter activity be-

Thin-film synthesis from reacting gases is attractive be-
cause of the extreme control one has over the growth me-
chanism and the morphology of the films. In the absence of
thermodynamic limitation and under laminar flow condi-
tions in the reactor, two different limiting regimes can be
discerned. First, at low substrate temperatures and high re-
actant concentrations, the film growth is limited by the rate
at which precursor molecules already adsorbed on the sur-
face react to form the solid product. This is the so-called
“reaction limited” regime. Second, at high substrate tem-
peratures and low reactant concentrations, the film growth
Fig. 1. Schematic drawing of the operational principle of dye-sensitized solar
cells. A nanostructured TiO2 electrode is sensitized for sunlight absorption
by a monolayer of a chromophore. Upon excitation of this chromophore,
electron injection into the conduction band of TiO2 takes place. Reduction
of the chromophore is taken care of by redox species in the electrolyte. The
electrons flow through the external circuit towards the back electrode, where
reduction of redox species takes place.
is limited by the diffusion of precursor molecules from the
gas towards the surface through a stagnant gas layer. In this
case the growth is referred to as “diffusion limited”. It is
the diffusion-limited regime that generally leads to un-
stable growth and hence to rough surface morphologies,
usually in the form of dendrites.
With only titanium tetrachloride (TiCl4) and O2, an
amorphous deposition is found, in which Ti, O, and Cl
atoms are present. With this combination of precursors, Cl2
gas must be formed at the expense of O2, which encounters
a high activation barrier. The chloride contamination in the
product is high and crystalline titania cannot be obtained.
Alternatively, when TiCl4 and H2O are used, the overall
chemical reaction proceeds as given in Equation 1. In this
case crystalline titanium dioxide is obtained, but X-ray
analyses show that the product does not exhibit the normal
anatase TiO2 structure. A marked expansion of the lattice
is observed, which is related to a high contamination with
chloride. Since the ionic radii of O2– and Cl– are 132 pm
and 181 pm, respectively, lattice expansion occurs when Cl–
occupies O2– lattice positions. The lattice reaction given in
Equation 2 may take place simultaneously.
TiCl4 (g) + 2 H2O (g) ! TiO2 (s) + 4 HCl (g)
2 ClO· + H2O (g) + ½ O2 ↑+ 2 e′
Here the Kröger–Vink notation is used; SP
a chemical element or point defect with symbol S is located
on a lattice position where element P is expected, leading
to a local charge distortion of c. Since relative charges
rather than real charges are considered, c = × for a neutral
substitution, · for a relative positive charge unit, and ′ for a
relative negative charge unit. The symbol for an excess
electron is e′ and at room temperature these electrons will
be located in the conduction band. In other words, lattice
reaction Equation 1 says that an HCl molecule can dissoci-
ate, leading to chloride dissolved into the titanium dioxide
lattice. In this lattice chlorine is present as Cl– located at an
oxide ion position. Since a Cl– ion on a O2– position has a
relative positive charge, additional conduction band elec-
trons are present to maintain overall charge neutrality. The
rate of reaction 1 is very high, so that either a diffusion lim-
itation is found or homogeneous nucleation in the gas
phase takes place with the formation of powder. In general,
with TiCl4, the adhesion of the deposits to the substrate
surface is poor. Accordingly, the performance of TiO2 pre-
pared from TiCl4 is poor and cannot be improved by heat
In contrast to TiCl4, in titanium tetra-isopropoxide,
Ti(OCH(CH3)2)4 (TTIP), both titanium and oxygen are
present, hence it can be used without additional oxygen re-
actant. However, the addition of O2 or H2O acts to en-
hance the reaction rate. Compared to TiCl4, the decompo-
sition of TTIP in the presence of water is much slower, and
the diffusion-limited regime for the latter is reached at
higher substrate temperatures. With TTIP, smooth and
dense anatase TiO2 films are usually obtained, i.e., reaction
These findings reveal that gas-phase synthesis using either
TiCl4 or TTIP does not lead to nanostructured TiO2. There-
fore, our strategy was changed; the CVD conditions were
modified to a new “mixed regime”. A combination of TiCl4
and TTIP injected into the CVD reactor together with a mix-
ture of O2 and H2O was studied, yielding surprising results.
The substrate temperature turned out to be critical, and val-
ues between 300 and 350 °C were selected. With these specific
combinations of reactants, the mixed regime (“diffusion lim-
ited” for TiCl4 and “reaction limited” for TTIP) is obtained.
It is this mixed regime that leads to beautiful anatase TiO2
fractals in the shape of trees, bushes, and grass.
Figures 2a–k show a collection of the typical morpholo-
gies that have been obtained in the mixed regime. The fol-
lowing conditions apply to the micrographs. Figures 2a–e
are taken from a single run in which TTIP and TiCl4 were
injected in a 2:3 ratio. Both H2O and O2 are present (see
Table 1), the duration of the deposition is 2 h and the sub-
strate temperature is 300 °C. Figure 2a is a grazing angle
micrograph of a “grass”-shaped deposit. An isolated
“plant” starts to grow, as is magnified in Figure 2b. A top
view of the film is shown in Figure 2c, and on the edge the
full shape of the pillars is shown in Figure 2d. The height of
this grass is about 15 µm. In Figure 2e, the first appearance
of a new structure on top of the “grass” is revealed.
When the growth is allowed to continue for 4 h instead
of 2 h, while the same conditions in the CVD reactor are
maintained, a multilayer “rain forest” morphology arises as
presented in Figure 2f. A cross section through the whole
film is shown. The series shown in Figures 2g–j shows the
depositions at 350 °C under various process parameters. In
Figure 2g, and its magnification Figure 2h, the precursor
concentrations are identical to those in the series shown in
Figures 2a–e, but the deposition time is extended to 3 h.
Now, “trees” of 150 µm height grow from the “grass-like”
surface of 15 µm height until they topple over under their
own weight. In Figure 2i the deposition time is reduced to
2 h and the TiCl4 concentration is halved. In Figure 2j the
deposition time is 2 h, the TiCl4 concentration has its origi-
nal value, but only O2, not H2O, is injected into the reactor.
Substantial variations in the morphology, due to relatively
minor changes in the process conditions, are observed. At
400 °C and 1 h deposition time, but with the same reactant
concentrations as in series shown in Figures 2a–h, micro-
crystallites form as shown in Figure 2k. Under these condi-
tions the reaction rate is high, and very thick films form
when the deposition time is extended.
Although it is not yet possible to establish the exact rela-
tionships between the CVD conditions and the TiO2 mor-
phology, the following general trends are found. At 300 °C
Chem. Vap. Deposition 1998, 4, No. 3
WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 1998
0948-1907/98/0305-0111 $ 17.50+.50/0
WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 1998
0948-1907/98/0305-0112 $ 17.50+.50/0
Chem. Vap. Deposition 1998, 4, No. 3
and 2 h deposition time, the population of fractal “trees” is
larger than that obtained at 400 °C. At this higher tempera-
ture a few of the trees grow at the expense of their neigh-
bors. In the CVD reactor there is competition for diffusing
precursor molecules and, by disturbing the stagnant gas
layer, higher “trees” are able to catch more of the reac-
tants. There is also a significant time effect. Growth for 1 h
at 300 °C leads to a dense packing of “grass”-shaped frac-
tals; no “trees” grow within this first hour. If the deposition
is allowed to continue for 4 h, a multilayer structure with
tree-, bush-, and grass-like fractals is found.
Takahashi et al.[8] recognized that, in a CVD process, the
crystal modifications of TiO2 are controlled kinetically
rather than thermodynamically. Different CVD precursors,
such as isopropyl titanate, ethyl titanate and tert-butyl tita-
nate give different regimes for rutile, anatase, and brookite
Fig 2a–k. Scanning electron micrographs of differ-
ent fractal morphologies of anatase TiO2 obtained
with CVD. For details, see the main text. a–e) A ser-
ies taken from a single batch. f) A cross section
through the whole film showing the multilayer “rain
forest” morphology. g–j) A series showing the de-
positions at 350 °C under various process param-
eters. Substantial variation in the morphology is ob-
served due to relatively minor changes in the
process conditions. k) Microcrystallites formed at
400 °C and 1 h deposition time.
formation. Isopropyl titanate gives mixed anatase/rutile de-
positions in a low-pressure CVD setup.[8c] Upon prolonged
deposition; a morphology change from a columnar to a
fractal structure is reported. the columns are anatase TiO2
and the fractal top layer is rutile. Their findings are inter-
preted as a strong epitaxial preferential growth of rutile.
Once rutile is formed on the anatase surface, a marked en-
hancement of the rutile reaction rate takes place. Our ex-
periments also lead to fractal TiO2, but now exclusively
anatase TiO2 is formed. The titanium precursor, titanium
tetra-isopropoxide, differs from that of Takahashi et al. and
our deposition temperature of 300–350 °C is 100 °C lower
than theirs. Nevertheless, it is remarkable that such a strong
relationship between the precursors and the kinetic control
of the morphology exists in the CVD of TiO2.
Photovoltaic solar cells, based on dye-sensitization of
TiO2 layers with the above morphologies and using cis-
(NCS)2-bis(4,4-dicarboxy-2,2-bipyridine)-ruthenium(II)
dye give reasonable efficiencies of about 3 % provided that
the films are sufficiently thick, i.e., thicker than 30 µm.
Since our films do not consist of nanoparticles but are
nanostructured fractal type, a thicker film than those used
by Grätzel’s group is necessary for the highest efficiencies.
In our case the optimal films thickness is found to be about
50 µm, which is about five times the optimum thickness in
the case of particulate electrodes. Nanostructured TiO2
films composed of sintered particles have a poor mechani-
cal stability when their thickness exceeds 50 µm. Moreover,
due to the numerous grain boundaries encountered by the
migrating electrons, current densities in these thick films
are low. In contrast, the present fractal films do not consist
of connected nanoparticles, and grain boundaries are,
therefore, expected to have less influence. In Figure 3 the
current–voltage behavior of a typical cell with the RuII
MLTC sensitizer is shown. This PV cell makes use of a frac-
tal TiO2 photoelectrode, similar to the one shown in Fig-
ure 2f, in a sandwich construction (see Fig. 1). Typical
values obtained with AM 1.5 irradiation (1000 W m–2) are:
open circuit photovoltage VOC = 0.6 V, short circuit current
ISC = 9 mA cm–2 (geometrical area), and the fill factor FF =
0.5. Reducing the light intensity to 500 W m–2 has no signif-
icant influence on the cell efficiency. We realize that the
present PV cells are less efficient than the ones reported by
Grätzel and further optimization is therefore necessary. In
particular, the surface enhancement factor in our elec-
trodes, even at 50 µm thickness, is below that of the most
successful particulate electrodes. Moreover, the dye, the
electrolyte solution, and the cell construction can be
improved. Although, in this study, fractal-type anatase
TiO2 is tested for possible use in dye-sensitized solar cells,
other applications as mentioned in the introduction may
also benefit from CVD-grown nanostructured anatase. It is
anticipated that optimal structure–property relationships
exist for different fields of application. With the versatility
of CVD it should be possible to reach this optimum.
Improving control over the morphologies and their repro-
ducibility is the target of our current investigations.
CVD is an attractive technique for the synthesis of ana-
tase TiO2 with a wide range of morphologies. Fractal struc-
tures can be made in a morphology that reflects the design
of trees, bushes, and grass. These structures are effective at
harvesting sunlight. With monolayer dye coverage, the
large surface area of 50 µm thick films is sufficient to col-
lect a substantial fraction of the sunlight. However, to
reach 100 % collection efficiency with monolayer dye cov-
erage, fractal-type films are still in need of improvement.
Since the generated electrons do not encounter as many
grain boundaries as in particulate electrodes, effective col-
lection of the electrons is possible. Gas-phase synthesis of
nanostructured anatase TiO2 allows optimization of the
structure–property relationships for a wide variety of appli-
cations. With regard to dye-sensitized solar cells, as nature
exemplifies, it is the architecture of fractal-shaped vegeta-
tion that is the ultimate morphology for efficient solar en-

The employed MOCVD reactor was a commercial cold-wall system
(Electro Gas Systems model J538) with a 60 mm diameter horizontal quartz
tube. Heating of the substrate was by means of two 1000 W tungsten halo-
gen lamps, allowing fast heating and cooling. During the CVD process, tem-
perature and gas flows were accurately controlled. The following liquid re-
actants were used: titanium tetra-isopropoxide, Ti(OCH(CH3)2)4 (TTIP, re-
agent grade >97 %, Aldrich), titanium tetrachloride (TiCl4, >98 %, Fluka),
water (H2O, Millipore, resistivity > 18.5 MΩ), and oxygen (O2 > 99.998 %).
Ultra pure nitrogen carrier gas (obtained from evaporation of liquid N2)
was fed through the liquid reactants which were heated to 120 °C for TTIP,
70 °C for TiCl4, and 50 °C for H2O. The gases were brought to saturation by
reflux coolers held 10 °C below the temperature of the reactants. In all cases
the lower temperature of the reflux coolers ensured saturation of the vapor.
The saturated vapor pressures were 50.47 torr for TTIP, 70.39 torr for TiCl4,
and 55.32 torr for H2O. Subsequently the vapors were mixed with 340 L h–1
N2 bulk flow at 1 bar and fed through stainless steel tubes kept at 120 °C.
When required, oxygen or H2O was introduced into the reactor via a differ-
ent entrance, to suppress TiO2 formation in the metal tubing. The substrate
temperature varied between 300 and 400 °C and was uniform over the sub-
strate to within 1 °C. The TTIP and TiCl4 partial pressures were calculated
from literature data [9,10] and are collected in Table 1 together with the
other relevant CVD parameters. As substrate material, fluorine-doped
SnO2 (F-SnO2, Libbey Owens Ford) with a sheet resistance of 20 Ω& was
used. After deposition the TiO2 samples were annealed in oxygen for 1 h at
X-ray spectra were recorded using a Philips diffractometer (Model PW
1840). Scanning electron micrographs were recorded with a JEOL JSM
Photoelectrochemical characterization was performed on sandwich-type
solar cells, consisting of a dyed, nanostructured TiO2 electrode, mechani-
cally clamped to a platinized F-SnO2 counter electrode. The dye used was
cis-(NCS)2-bis(4,4-dicarboxy-2,2-bipyridine)-ruthenium(II) purchased from
Solaronix SA. This is an MLTC complex provided with carboxyl groups for
bonding to the TiO2 surface. The cell was filled using capillary forces with a
redox electrolyte composed of 0.5 M KI and 0.05 M I2 in a mixture of ethy-
lene carbonate (75 %) and propylene carbonate (25 %). The optical system
consisted of a Xe lamp, monochromator (Acton Research, Model 275),
lenses, and high-pass filters and was calibrated for the optical throughput.
Voltages and currents were controlled and recorded using an electrochemi-
cal interface (Schlumberger, Model 1286).
